what_it_does: This file is a desktop HTML/CSS mockup for the D7 self-modification revert/rollback/audit panel. It renders a full application shell (toolbar, left rail, canvas, footer, and a right-hand Workshop audit panel) and uses CSS from ../design-system.css. The mockup visually separates features that are already built (a single most-recent self-change row with a revert button) from features that are only proposed (a multi-entry self-change ledger, per-entry changed-files, conflict warnings for non-tip reverts). It also includes extensive comments mapping each behavior to real backend code locations and noting that D7 has no registered ui:// address of its own.
observations: Static HTML/CSS mockup with no JavaScript; buttons are inert.
App grid layout with top, rail, canvas, foot, and a 460px right-side panel.
Right panel is framed as the Workshop/self-modification audit surface.
A legend and per-block badges distinguish EXISTS (built) from PROPOSED.
The EXISTS block shows one latest self-change ('add node-type sentiment') with a sha, [self-apply] tag, and a revert button.
The PROPOSED ledger block shows older self-change rows, a reverted state, and per-row revert buttons.
A PROPOSED diff block lists changed files for the sentiment commit.
An activity strip references ui://activity and shows revert/apply/grow events.
An address strip notes that D7 has no registered ui:// address and proposes ui://workshop/self-changes.
Comments cite suite.py, bridge.py, and App.tsx line numbers for grounding.
standouts: The visible EXISTS/PROPOSED spine is the organizing principle of the whole mockup.
The built surface is intentionally thin: only the latest self-change plus one revert control.
It openly admits that 'audit' is mostly proposed and that the data already exists in git.
It flags a real product gap: no registered ui:// address for SM-revert/D7.
conventions: Follows the shared design-system.css variable naming and app-grid layout.
Uses data-ui-ref attributes to mark UI surfaces for addressability.
Heavy inline documentation linking visual elements to backend methods and endpoints.
Breaks typical mockup convention by prominently labeling which parts are not yet built.
concerns: Proposed UI blocks visually dominate the panel; a reader could miss the badges and assume the ledger is shipped.
The built revert action is described as immediate with no confirm or preview, which is a safety concern.
Sample data (sha, timestamps, filenames) is hardcoded and illustrative only.
No interaction or accessibility affordances are wired.
notes: A design mockup that doubles as a grounded spec, clearly separating the one real self-mod revert control from a proposed full audit ledger.
questions: Is this mockup meant for implementation handoff or for design review only?
Which proposed features are scheduled, and which are exploratory?
How should the UI handle a git conflict when reverting a non-tip commit?
Are all referenced CSS variables actually defined in design-system.css?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose (D7 self-mod revert/rollback/audit desktop draft) matches the content: it shows the built revert control and a proposed audit ledger. The word 'audit' is aspirational, but the file labels that honestly.
apparent_themes: Honest design documentation: existing vs proposed
intention_signals: The file is trying to design a trustworthy, inspectable interface for system self-edits, making it clear what the operator can undo today while sketching a richer future audit ledger over the existing git substrate.
novelty: It treats the mockup as a spec document: every visual block is badged as built or proposed, mapped to real code lines, and the missing registered address is flagged rather than invented.
contribution: A visual vocabulary and layout for distinguishing shipped self-mod controls from a future audit ledger, plus explicit backend-to-UI mappings and a clear flag that a ui:// address for D7 needs to be registered.
summary_for_embedding: A desktop HTML mockup for the D7 self-modification audit panel that shows the built latest-self-change revert control and a proposed full self-change ledger, with extensive code-grounding comments and an honest exists-vs-proposed badge system.
intention_for_embedding: To design a bounded, reversible self-modification audit surface while clearly separating already-built behavior from proposed extensions.
